mnemonics.title: Wait Macros Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: WIF EXITed = Normal Exit; WIF SIGNALed = Signal Kill; WIF STOPped = Job Control; WEXIT STATUS = Exit Code; WTERM SIG = Signal Number

mnemonics.title: WNOHANG = No Hang
mnemonics.content: WNOHANG: Don't hang (block), return immediately if no child exited - like checking phone for messages without waiting

acronyms.expansion: Wait If - macro prefix meaning 'check if' the condition is true
acronyms.usage: WIFEXITED checks if exited normally, WIFSIGNALED checks if killed by signal

acronyms.expansion: Wait NO HANG - do not block/hang waiting
acronyms.usage: Used in waitpid() options to return immediately if no child has exited
Option to waitpid() to also report processes stopped by signals (job control)

acronyms.expansion: Wait EXIT STATUS - extract exit code
acronyms.usage: Gets the exit code (0-255) when process terminated normally

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: Parent forks → Child runs → Child exits (becomes zombie) → Parent calls wait() → Zombie removed → Parent continues. Show the zombie state in between.
Use a decision tree: Is WNOHANG set? → Yes: Return 0 immediately / No: Block. Is specific pid? → Yes: Wait for that pid / No: Wait for any child (-1).
Draw 16-bit status word: Upper bits = exit status (8 bits), Lower bits = signal info (7 bits) + core dump flag (1 bit). Show how macros extract different portions.

analogies.analogy: Like a person who has died but their paperwork hasn't been filed. The person (process) is gone, but their record (zombie) still exists until someone (parent) completes the bureaucracy (calls wait()).
Like waiting at an airport gate for a specific flight. You sit and wait (block) until your flight arrives (child terminates). You can't do anything else until your flight comes.
Like checking your phone for messages occasionally while doing other work, instead of sitting by the phone waiting (blocking). You get back to work if no new messages.
Like children whose parents die being adopted by relatives (init process). The children (orphan processes) continue running but their new guardian (init) periodically checks on them.
